perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall, upon termination of this Agreement, or upon written request of Discloser, whichever is earlier, immediately, but not later than 10 days after any notice thereof by Discloser, return (or destroy at Discloser’s option) all copies of such Discloser’s Confidential Information and certify in writing its compliance with this requirement, except that Recipient may retain a copy of such Confidential Information solely for archival purpose .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall, upon termination of this Agreement, or upon written request of Discloser, whichever is earlier, immediately, but not later than 60 days after any notice thereof by Discloser, return (or destroy at Discloser’s option) all copies of such Discloser’s Confidential Information and certify in writing its compliance with this requirement, except that Recipient may retain a copy of such Confidential Information solely for archival purpose .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline to 60 days could be problematic. Although this agreement is governed by Korean law, for the purposes of this exercise, if this contract were under United States law, such as in California, this could be considered too long under certain circumstances, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws which emphasize prompt action in protecting confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly defining 'reasonable time' for returning confidential information, the extended 60-day period could be deemed unreasonable in some cases, potentially hindering prompt legal action to protect trade secrets, violating the spirit of trade secret law. The prompt return of confidential information is critical to mitigating damages and maintaining trade secret status. California law emphasizes the need for timely action in protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 10 days to 60 days to return confidential information introduces an inconsistency that could contradict the spirit of trade secret law, specifically the need for timely action. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight concerns that a 60-day period might be deemed unreasonable and could hinder prompt legal action to protect trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CIV 3426.2) does not explicitly define 'reasonable time', it focuses on the ability to enjoin misappropriation of trade secrets and to take affirmative acts to protect a trade secret, which is undermined by a potentially long delay. The extended period weakens the ability to promptly mitigate damages.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective from the Effective Date until 24 months thereafter. Either party may terminate this Agreement for any or no reason upon 10 days written notice to the other party. However, any termination of this Agreement shall not relieve Recipient of its confidentiality and use obligations with respec t to Confidential Information disclosed prior to the date of such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective from the Effective Date until 24 months thereafter. Either party may terminate this Agreement for any or no reason upon 1 days written notice to the other party. However, any termination of this Agreement shall not relieve Recipient of its confidentiality and use obligations with respec t to Confidential Information disclosed prior to the date of such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 1 day might be problematic. While this agreement is governed by Korean law, for the purposes of this exercise, if this contract were under United States law, such as in California, such a short notice period could be deemed unconscionable or against public policy, especially if it causes undue hardship or surprise to the other party.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 allows courts to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. A one-day notice period might be considered unconscionable due to its potential for unfair surprise and the limited opportunity for the other party to prepare for the termination of the agreement.  This is particularly true if the agreement involves significant business operations or dependencies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 10 days to 1 day. The Law Explanation suggests this could be deemed unconscionable under California Civil Code Section 1670.5 due to the potential for unfair surprise. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CIV 1670.5, states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable. Therefore, the reduced notice period in the changed text directly contradicts the possibility of legal scrutiny for unconscionability, making the contradiction direct and potentially impactful.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient’s duty to protect Discloser’s Confidential Information expires 5 years from the date on which that Confidential Information was disclosed to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient’s duty to protect Discloser’s Confidential Information expires 1 year from the date on which that Confidential Information was disclosed to Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to 1 year may not be sufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable laws. While this agreement is governed by Korean law, for the purposes of this exercise, if this contract were under United States law, such as the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), it could be seen as an insufficient duration to safeguard trade secrets depending on the industry and nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to maintain trade secret status, especially for information with a longer competitive lifespan, potentially undermining the protections afforded by the DTSA. The reasonableness of the duration is fact-dependent, and one year may be inadequate for certain industries.

perturbation.justification: The modification of the contract text, reducing the confidentiality period from 5 years to 1 year, creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that 'A one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to maintain trade secret status, especially for information with a longer competitive lifespan, potentially undermining the protections afforded by the DTSA.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1836, discusses civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. While the snippet doesn't explicitly define a minimum confidentiality period, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that a one-year period may be inadequate to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent of laws like the DTSA to safeguard trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Given that the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition covers when a contract sets 'a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law', the changed text's inadequate confidentiality period could render the terms unenforceable or illegal, as it undermines the protection of trade secrets as intended by law.

perturbation.original_text: All disputes, controversies or claims between the parties arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (including its existence, validity or termination) shall be finally resolved by arbitration to be held in Seoul, Korea and conducted in English under the Rules of Arbitration of the Intern ational Chamber of Commerce ; provided, however, that each party may enforce its or its affiliates’ intellectual property rights in any court of competent jurisdiction, including but not limit ed to equitable relief .
perturbation.changed_text: All disputes, controversies or claims between the parties arising out of or in connection with this Agreement (including its existence, validity or termination) shall be finally resolved by arbitration to be held in Pyongyang, North Korea and conducted in English under the Rules of Arbitration of the Intern ational Chamber of Commerce ; provided, however, that each party may enforce its or its affiliates’ intellectual property rights in any court of competent jurisdiction, including but not limit ed to equitable relief .
perturbation.explanation: Changing the arbitration location to Pyongyang, North Korea, could create enforceability issues under U.S. law. While this agreement is governed by Korean law, for the purposes of this exercise, if a US company were party to this agreement, this could be problematic, as U.S. courts may be hesitant to enforce arbitration agreements requiring proceedings in a country with a legal system significantly different from the U.S., particularly if the location raises concerns about fairness and impartiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S. Code § 201
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act favors arbitration agreements. However, U.S. courts retain the power to review arbitration awards and may refuse to enforce them if they violate public policy or are rendered in a manner inconsistent with due process.  Arbitration in North Korea could raise significant concerns about due process and fairness, potentially leading a U.S. court to refuse enforcement. This undermines the intent and purpose of the FAA.  While the FAA favors arbitration, it does not compel enforcement of agreements that are fundamentally unfair or contrary to U.S. public policy.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies arbitration in Pyongyang, North Korea, which raises concerns about fairness and due process under U.S. law. The Law Explanation states that U.S. courts may refuse to enforce arbitration awards if they violate public policy or due process. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both refer to 9 U.S. Code § 201, enforcing the Convention on the Recognition and Enforcement of Foreign Arbitral Awards. While the FAA favors arbitration, arbitration in North Korea could be seen as contrary to U.S. public policy. The change directly affects the enforceability of the arbitration agreement, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be delivered by electronic mail communications in pdf format, and pdf copies of executed signature pages shall be binding as originals. Each party to this Agreement agrees to use electronic signatures; and be subject to the provisions of the U.S. E -SIGN Act (i.e., the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (ESIGN, Pub.L. 106 -229, 14 Stat. 464, enacted June 30, 2000, 15 U .S.C. ch. 96).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be delivered by electronic mail communications in pdf format, and pdf copies of executed signature pages shall be binding as originals. Each party to this Agreement agrees to use electronic signatures; and shall NOT be subject to the provisions of the U.S. E -SIGN Act (i.e., the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (ESIGN, Pub.L. 106 -229, 14 Stat. 464, enacted June 30, 2000, 15 U .S.C. ch. 96).
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the agreement is not subject to the E-SIGN Act creates uncertainty and potential legal issues regarding the validity of electronic signatures.  While this agreement is governed by Korean law, for the purposes of this exercise, if a US company were party to this agreement, this could be problematic, as it attempts to circumvent federal law regarding electronic signatures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-SIGN Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The E-SIGN Act generally validates electronic signatures for transactions in or affecting interstate or foreign commerce. Explicitly stating that the agreement is NOT subject to the E-SIGN Act could render the electronic signatures unenforceable, conflicting with the Act's purpose of promoting the use of electronic signatures. The Act provides a baseline for validity, and attempting to exclude it raises questions about the parties' intent and the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that the agreement is NOT subject to the U.S. E-SIGN Act, while the law explanation states the E-SIGN Act generally validates electronic signatures. This contradicts the purpose of the E-SIGN Act and could render the electronic signatures unenforceable. The Perturbation explanation also confirms the original text was modified to remove this protection.
